what_it_does: This file records an adversarial source-pixel verification pass over a content-budgets face: it opens representative slide images, adjudicates whether each typographic slot behaviour is pixel-true, and issues verdicts (stands, corrected, refuted) while flagging fabricated stated-rules and confirming-bias augmentations.
observations: Only 4 of 293 samples in the underlying findings carry any note.
The reviewer opened 10 representative slides across capital-raise, pitch-deck, recent-pitches, vi-onepager and landing mockups.
Verdict counts: 7 slots stand, 5 are corrected, and 2 are refuted.
Five verdicts are flagged as confirming_bias because pixel-true behaviour was paired with invented corpus rules.
Quoted rules for node-label, button-cta, bullet, and section-head are identified as fabricated; no matching note exists in the 293-sample findings and no slide prints such rules.
The slide-title empirical max=86ch is refuted as a misclassified body/tagline sample; the real landing hero title is ~40ch one line.
Decision-surface slots (option-label, option-implication) are refuted because they admit zero source samples and are explicitly inferred placeholders.
standouts: Behaviour true, provenance faked: every one-line/multi-line slot behaviour is pixel-confirmed, but several augmentations cite typographic rules that do not exist in the source.
The findings contain exactly four real notes, yet the proposal quotes many more stated rules than are present.
The 86ch slide-title outlier is reclassified from hero title to subtitle/body tagline.
Confirming-bias failures are corrected rather than refuted, preserving the observed behaviour while stripping fabricated citations.
conventions: Follows a structured ledger format with face, method, slides_opened, verdicts, counts, and headline fields.
Each verdict names the slot, states claimed status, gives a verdict, cites source evidence, and optionally provides a corrected recipe and a confirming_bias flag.
Uses adversarial verification language and explicitly distinguishes what a JPG can adjudicate (recurrence, line behaviour, wrapping) from what it cannot (median-across-N, glyph-advance, px formulas).
Breaks typical audit convention by embedding a bias diagnosis (confirming_bias) inside the verdict object.
concerns: Char-count estimates (e.g., ~60-68ch) are approximate and derived by eye from slides, not measured.
The method states JPGs cannot measure median or glyph-advance, yet some verdicts still rely on inferred caps and ranges.
The confirming_bias flag conflates honest inference with outright fabrication; the distinction is asserted rather than demonstrated.
Refuted inferred slots are retained as placeholders, which could still leak into downstream systems if not guarded.
The audit depends on a manual, non-reproducible slide-opening process.
notes: A pixel-level audit that keeps the observed typographic behaviour but strips out invented source citations and misclassified outliers.
questions: What is the upstream proposal or findings file being verified?
Who is 'Tim' mentioned as the recipient of inferred slots?
How are character counts and line breaks actually measured from JPGs?
What downstream system consumes these corrected recipes?
How does this face relate to the type-lane formulas it imports but does not adjudicate?
What does 'face' signify in this ledger?
purpose_vs_actual: The file acts as a verification audit and largely matches that purpose, though it also functions as a bias-correction report that rewrites recipes rather than only accepting or rejecting them.
intention_signals: The file is trying to stop a design-system proposal from encoding hallucinated typographic laws by re-anchoring each content slot in observable slide behaviour, while preserving useful but unsupported inferences as explicitly labelled placeholders.
novelty: It is unusual for a verification artifact to enumerate the exact count of real notes in the source findings and to explicitly label confirming-bias fabrications, correcting the behaviour while refuting the citation.
contribution: It contributes a slot-level separation of pixel-true behaviour from fabricated provenance, a set of corrected recipes, explicit bias flags, an inventory of real versus invented notes, and a clear refutation of unsupported inferred slots.
summary_for_embedding: A JSON verification audit of a content-budgets typographic face that confirms observed one-line and multi-line slot behaviours across slide images but corrects fabricated corpus citations and refutes unsupported inferred decision-surface slots.
intention_for_embedding: To adversarially verify content-budget slot rules against source slides, preserving behaviour while removing invented stated-rules and misclassified outliers.
